sentences.text: My name is Allen Donald Goddard.
G-O-D-D-A-R-D.
I was born with the name Gutherz, G-U-T-H-E-R-Z.
Dolek.
D-O-L-E-K.
And Jewish name is [?
Abraham ?], [INAUDIBLE].
My birth date is the 22nd November, 1915.
Next month-- oh, no.
Sorry.
This month I will be 81.
I was born in Cracow, Poland.
C-R-A-C-O-V or K-R-A-K-O-W.
Cracow.
My father's name was Leon, L-E-O-N.
His surname was Gutherz, G-U-T-H-E-R-Z.
He was a private-- private firm employed.
As bookkeeper, salesman.
He was well-oriented in politics, in social work.
But generally speaking, he was a old soldier.
He spent 25 years in the Austro-Hungarian army and then in Polish army.
My mother was always at home.
Her name was Gustawa, G-U-S-T-A-W-A, or Gusti.
And the Jewish name was Gitla, Gitla-- G-I-T-L-A.
I had a brother, Joseph and Henry.
All younger.
They're the youngest in the family.
Very close.
Yes.
We-- we lived in a flat, generally speaking.
My father was a private employer, employed people.
We couldn't buy any houses.
Our finances were minor.
No, never.
No.
Generally it was the Polish language, but my father and my mother spoke very fluently in German language, and little bit in Jewish.
Oh yes.
My father was a professional soldier, so he expected that we are always at his arm lengths and fulfilling his orders.
My father was really brought up in Germany, so he wasn't a great believer.
But when he married my mother, he promised the grandfather that he would help to bring children in Jewish faith, and he always adhered to.
He always went with me to the synagogue in Cracow, in temple where I had a chance to bless, because we are kohanim.
My mother was kosher, yes.
Yes.
We were members for years in Temple Cracow.
It was a Orthodox, yes.
Originally my mother wanted to enroll me in the Hebrew school, but unfortunately, we couldn't allow ourself our fees.
So I was attending the public school for four years.
And after that, I was enrolled into the college, the first-- the oldest college in Poland, a classic one with Latin and Greek language.
And then I went to the university.
No.
That was separate one.
Yes, we had religious instructions.
In the primary schools, we had, I think, if my memory serves me well, the name of the teacher was Hochman.
But in the college we received the religious instructions from Dr.
Schmelkes.
He was well-known person for all colleges in Cracow.
Oh yes.
We had a lot of friends because we are always a minority.
In the college, I remember there were four Jews and 35 non-Jews, so we had to.
And we were always in a very good friendly matter.
I can't remember this.
But we were participating in all public holidays, whether they were in the school or outside the school.
We were members of the society.
Oh, it is hard to say especially one subject, because we were compared to pass examination.
Probably the most which appealed to me was the German language, because it was easy to receive from my parents the information.
But the Jewish language was, for me, 100%-- nearly 100%-- understandable, but I couldn't converse.
I couldn't speak to the people in Jewish, but I understood.
By that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was trying to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not to be in one only way.
First of all was the literature.
I had a chance to be a member of so-called literary club, where we always commented on the new addition of books.
The second interest was I organized a school shop with stationary on a cheaper way to save some money for my friends.
The third idea was, because I liked to fly, but only in my dreams.
We had a club in our school, and we collected money for gliders.
There were only two schools who were interested in gliders.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of them was our school, and because we collected a lot of money for the first glider, we received a free flight from Cracow to Warsaw.
I was the happy one.
Oh, that was in the year 1935, shortly before my matriculation.
In some cases.
There was one or two cases that professors sometimes gave us idea that he's not our friend.
But generally speaking, that wasn't allowed.
Officially, there was no antisemitism.
But when we came to the university, it was a much stronger problem, because there were days without Jews.
Once I came to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] university, I took part in the lecture.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Suddenly a bell stopped a professor of-- of his lecture and, from the back, came one student and told me, friend, today is a day without Jews.
So I had to leave it.
But I knew that there were excesses.
There were some violence in the streets.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a Nazi-- one Nazi type of party in Poland, the Nationals.
And they had the chance sometimes, unofficially, to make some perturbance-- turbulence I would say.
No.
No.
First of all, we were too young, and univer-- these colleges did not allow us to take part or belong to any political clubs.
But we had our Jewish connections, especially with a type of early Zionist.
I belonged to the student's corporation, they're called, El Al, when I was in the college.
And later on, when I went to the university, I advanced to El Al-- to [INAUDIBLE], which was our [INAUDIBLE] Zionist organization-- corporation.
Very little.
Very little.
My mother always dreamt, but we didn't see any possibility by that time.
Later on, there was a chance.
I know that one of our-- of my boss was visiting-- visited by underground Haganah, but I don't know how that was finished.
No.
When the war commenced, everything stopped.
Strictly speaking, my studies finished in March, 1939, but it all started in 1935 when I applied to the Cracow university to study medicine.
Unfortunately, there was a system in Poland, which was called numerus clausus, which means that 10% of seats were reserved for Jews.
And because there were 100 seats in the first year, so only 10 Jews could be accepted.
But priority had sons of doctors.
And, of course, I failed.
So I picked studies very close to the medicine-- medical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] faculty, and next year I applied again.
By that time the numerus clausus was cut to 7%, and the same has happened next year.
When I applied during the third year, there came a numerus nullus, which means no Jews could study, whether he was the son of a doctor or not.
Oh yes.
We-- by that time we have all information by radio, by press, and some commentaries in the society.
We knew what's coming, but we couldn't do anything else only to follow what the government decided.
There were several parts.
There's usually leftist, rightist, and centrists.
I belonged to the rightists.
But the future has shown that, because of that split, we were not a strong society whatsoever.
We had a representative into Polish, same-- how do you call it? legal representation after the elections.
It was Dr.
Osais Thon, well-known personality.
How do you spell it please?
T-H-O-N.
By the way, I have to mentions that Dr.
Osais Thon was [INAUDIBLE] for my brother, Joseph.
My father liked very much the doctor, and we were in very close contact.
My friends, my colleagues.
I had a very nice [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] group of people.
We understood each others and we were studying the Jewish history.
We had to pass examination.
And everybody had a nickname.
Funny or not funny, I was having a nickname Juen Juen-- J-U-E-N, because I had a [INAUDIBLE].
I had a- something what my friends didn't like it, but that made me popular.
First of all, we had meetings.
Then we had some sort of entertainment.
Then we had some collections of different cases.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was always-- a directive came from the top.
I wouldn't say that they were religious, but they were very sympathizing with Jewish faith.
But not all were practicing.
They had to belong somewhere.
You had to have some ideas in that time.
You can't say that you are not believing, but everybody was practicing what he understood was in his reach.
Oh yes.
Yes, yes.
We all belonged to the Zionist organization.
We always paid some membership.
Once, I lost my book with the receipts for my membership, and it was picked up by one of my professors, a woman, Jewish lady.
I remember her name, Arnold.
She picked it up and she asked me that my mother should come to her, and she said if that would be found by another professor, you would be expelled from the college.
You are not allowed to belong to anything like this.
Oh, we read the papers and we heard the radio.
There was no television by that time, but press informed us with the special editions.
And there were some meetings in some places.
We know, in Cracow was a very strong Socialist party.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But all other parties, whether it was conservative or Nationals, what they say, because the Polish population was also divided with their ideas, with their orientation.
First of all, all shops were closed.
All life stopped because we did not know what will be the life like under the occupied.
And after all, the occupied immediately took way by bringing big resolutions and information and penalties if you won't follow.
We were ordered to open shops, to do the normal activities.
And generally speaking, they infiltrated in our life very deeply.
Yes.
By that time, it was rather-- everybody was looking for himself.
And if there was somebody who wanted to help you, it was no difference.
But later on, after certain time, when Nazis came with their ideas, we started to feel that the Polish population is not very friendly.
But a great number of people were helping Jews.
Of course, it was always not only friendships.
It was always a bribe because Jews had a chance to secure their future.
Oh yes.
Yes, we were.
First of all there were edicts that we can't live in that part of Cracow.
And eventually, they organized a Jewish center, which was called the ghetto.
In the beginning it was open, but later on, they closed it.
I can't forget the date when the ghetto was closed.
It was the 21st of March, 1941.
We were not allowed to live in the ghetto.
We were lived outside the ghetto, but we had to come to the rabbi and to the ghetto to be married, to get the certificate, and later on we had to go back outside Cracow.
No.
We were first in our normal apartments in Cracow, but then we had to rent some outside Cracow in some suburbs, I would say, not very far because we wanted to keep contact with our workplace.
But later on, when they closed the ghetto, there were only commandos surrounded by Ukrainian or German soldiers.
We went to work, and in the same way, they accompanied us when we wanted to come back home.

We were not allowed to-- because we had our armband with a David's star-- we can't-- in the beginning, we were not allowed to even to go to public restaurants, or we didn't-- we couldn't use public transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The life became, from day to day, more and more difficult.
No.
No.
I was working in the private firm that was a general representation of Omega and Tissot watches.
And before the war broke out, we worked in big cities only, and they were also travelers for smaller areas.
But later on, when the Germany occupied Poland, all our traveling stopped.
We could only work in the office.
I worked in the office about 23, 25 people, a mixture of Jewish and non-Jewish.
Of course, majority was Jewish.
There were groups that had permits, but only-- there were also single people who received for some purpose.
They were represented-- representative of Poland, of Jewish and Polish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trade and commerce.
Yes, they were.
But they were limited, you know.
Oh, probably Gestapo.
That was the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] organization, a Jewish-- how to call this?
Representation, who contacted the German and they received the-- because in the beginning, it was not regulated.
But later on, everybody got some rations, and we had to produce the ration if you wanted to buy in the shop.
I couldn't give you now immediately the names, but it was, generally speaking, the-- the head of the SS or German police-- Polizei, Polizei.
In the beginning it was very, very difficult.
You were-- you couldn't find a place where only one family lived.
We were in a special position to got-- to get a room in our friend's place, so we could say that we were together with them.
But there were cases where two, three, or four, sometimes, families had to divide their room with partition, and where children were, it wasn't a easy living condition at all.
What was-- proper--
Oh, yes.
In the beginning, they allowed you, but then it was restricted because of transport and so on.
Yes.
I think ghetto was about 30,000 or 40,000 people, but later on it was dwindled.
When we went to the Plaszow camp, it was only 20,000 because the other part was all of it reduced for sending out to-- to concentration camps.
There were always deportations, because there were always reason to find people on the streets or at homes.
Yes, there were.
Probably it was the Jewish police-- poli-- policy to leave this alone to Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know how to call it.
Ordnungsdienst, that was called, which means, if we translate it, it was only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] service for keeping order.
But later on it was rather a reason to keep the German deportations.
Oh, yes.
No.
It was voluntary.
From Cracow Ghetto, we were transferred to Plaszow.
That was behind Cracow area, where it was really a Jewish cemetery.
Part of the cemetery was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of the cemetery was used for building barracks.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was the March, 1943, when really the ghetto was closed, or rather, all inhabitants were sent out to the places of Vernichtungslager, as they say.
